examples.explanation: Start at city 1 (index 1, location 3) with fuel 5. Finish is city 3 (index 3, location 8). Compute distances: |3-2|=1, |3-6|=3, |3-8|=5, |3-4|=1. From city 1: can move to city 0 (fuel 5-1=4), city 2 (5-3=2), city 3 (5-5=0), city 4 (5-1=4). Recursively: From city 0 (loc 2, fuel 4): distances to others: |2-3|=1, |2-6|=4, |2-8|=6, |2-4|=2. Moves: to city 1 (fuel 4-1=3), city 2 (4-4=0), city 4 (4-2=2). From city 1 (loc 3, fuel 3): moves to city 0 (3-1=2), city 2 (3-3=0), city 3 (3-5=-2 invalid), city 4 (3-1=2). Continue tracing all paths: Valid routes: 1->3 (direct), 1->0->1->3, 1->4->1->3, 1->2->1->3. Total 4 routes.

Start at city 1 (loc 3) with fuel 6, finish city 0 (loc 4). Distances: |3-4|=1, |3-1|=2. From city 1: move to city 0 (fuel 6-1=5) or city 2 (6-2=4). From city 0 (loc 4, fuel 5): finish reached, count 1. Also can move to city 1 (5-1=4) or city 2 (5-3=2). From city 2 (loc 1, fuel 4): move to city 0 (4-3=1) or city 1 (4-2=2). Continue: Valid routes: 1->0, 1->2->0, 1->2->1->0, 1->0->1->0, 1->0->1->2->0. Total 5 routes.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n^2 * fuel)
approaches.summary: Use memoization to store results for subproblems defined by the current city and remaining fuel, avoiding redundant calculations in the brute force recursion. This approach is more efficient and practical for moderate constraints.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Routes with Fuel Constraints Using DP Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting all possible routes from start to finish with fuel constraints using memoized DP.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all possible routes from a start city to a finish city given fuel constraints, where moving between cities consumes fuel equal to the absolute distance between them. The brute force approach would explore all possible paths, leading to exponential time complexity. The DP memoization approach optimizes this by storing results for subproblems defined by (current_city, remaining_fuel). The intuition is that many paths share the same intermediate states—reaching the same city with the same remaining fuel—and we can avoid redundant calculations by caching these results. The algorithm uses recursion with memoization: define a recursive function countRoutes(current, fuel) that returns the number of ways to reach finish from current with given fuel. Base cases: if fuel < 0, return 0; if current == finish, count this as 1 valid route (plus continue exploring). For each other city next, if next != current, recursively call countRoutes(next, fuel - abs(locations[current] - locations[next])) and sum results. Memoize results in a 2D array or dictionary. Time complexity is O(n² * fuel) because for each state (n * fuel possibilities), we iterate over up to n cities. Space complexity is O(n * fuel) for memoization storage. Connection to L1: This builds on the key recursive subroutine by adding memoization and handling the full problem logic. Common mistakes include not handling the case where start == finish (should count as 1 route even without moving), forgetting to mod results if required, and incorrect fuel consumption calculation. Real code pattern: Use @lru_cache or manual memo dictionary.

examples.trace: Start at city 1 (index 1, location 3) with fuel 5. Finish is city 3 (index 3, location 8). Compute distances: |3-2|=1, |3-6|=3, |3-8|=5, |3-4|=1. From city 1: can move to city 0 (fuel 5-1=4), city 2 (5-3=2), city 3 (5-5=0), city 4 (5-1=4). Recursively: From city 0 (loc 2, fuel 4): distances to others: |2-3|=1, |2-6|=4, |2-8|=6, |2-4|=2. Moves: to city 1 (fuel 4-1=3), city 2 (4-4=0), city 4 (4-2=2). From city 1 (loc 3, fuel 3): moves to city 0 (3-1=2), city 2 (3-3=0), city 3 (3-5=-2 invalid), city 4 (3-1=2). Continue tracing all paths: Valid routes: 1->3 (direct), 1->0->1->3, 1->4->1->3, 1->2->1->3. Total 4 routes.

Start at city 1 (loc 3) with fuel 6, finish city 0 (loc 4). Distances: |3-4|=1, |3-1|=2. From city 1: move to city 0 (fuel 6-1=5) or city 2 (6-2=4). From city 0 (loc 4, fuel 5): finish reached, count 1. Also can move to city 1 (5-1=4) or city 2 (5-3=2). From city 2 (loc 1, fuel 4): move to city 0 (4-3=1) or city 1 (4-2=2). Continue: Valid routes: 1->0, 1->2->0, 1->2->1->0, 1->0->1->0, 1->0->1->2->0. Total 5 routes.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with State (city, fuel)
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that counts routes from a given city with given fuel.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP approach: a recursive function countRoutes(current, fuel) that returns the number of ways to reach the finish city from the current city with the given remaining fuel. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems: from the current state, explore all possible next moves to other cities, reducing fuel accordingly, and sum the counts from those recursive calls. The base cases: if fuel < 0, return 0 (invalid state); if current == finish, we have found a valid route, so count 1 (but also continue exploring to find routes that pass through finish and return). The recursive step: initialize total = 0; for each other city next (0 to n-1, next != current), compute fuel_needed = abs(locations[current] - locations[next]); if fuel >= fuel_needed, add countRoutes(next, fuel - fuel_needed) to total. Return total. Time complexity for this function without memoization is exponential O(n^fuel) in worst case. Space complexity is O(fuel) for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called within the full solution, and memoization is added to cache results. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of state exploration. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle the case where start == finish (should return 1 even if fuel is 0), incorrect fuel comparison, or infinite recursion if not checking next != current. Real code pattern: Define function with parameters (current, fuel), use loops over cities.

examples.trace: Current city 0 (loc 2), fuel 4, finish city 2 (loc 6). Base: fuel >=0, current != finish. Explore next cities: city 1 (loc 3): fuel_needed = |2-3|=1, fuel remaining = 3, recurse countRoutes(1,3). city 2: fuel_needed = |2-6|=4, fuel remaining = 0, recurse countRoutes(2,0). For countRoutes(1,3): current=1 (loc 3), fuel=3. Explore: city 0: fuel_needed=1, remaining=2, recurse countRoutes(0,2). city 2: fuel_needed=3, remaining=0, recurse countRoutes(2,0). For countRoutes(0,2): current=0, fuel=2. Explore: city 1: fuel_needed=1, remaining=1, recurse countRoutes(1,1). city 2: fuel_needed=4, invalid. For countRoutes(1,1): current=1, fuel=1. Explore: city 0: fuel_needed=1, remaining=0, recurse countRoutes(0,0). city 2: fuel_needed=3, invalid. For countRoutes(0,0): current=0, fuel=0. Explore: city 1: fuel_needed=1, invalid; city 2: fuel_needed=4, invalid. Base: current != finish, return 0. Backtrack: countRoutes(1,1) gets 0 from city 0, so total=0. countRoutes(0,2) gets 0 from city 1, so total=0. countRoutes(1,3) gets 0 from city 0 and from city 2: countRoutes(2,0) has current==finish, return 1. So countRoutes(1,3)=1. countRoutes(0,4) gets 1 from city 1 and from city 2: countRoutes(2,0) returns 1. Total = 1+1=2.

Current city 0 (loc 1), fuel 1, finish city 1 (loc 2). Base: fuel >=0, current != finish. Explore next: city 1: fuel_needed = |1-2|=1, fuel remaining = 0, recurse countRoutes(1,0). For countRoutes(1,0): current=1==finish, return 1. So countRoutes(0,1) = 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Definition and Transition in DP
ladders.desc: Define the DP state as (city, fuel) and transitions between states.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of this approach is to define the problem state as a pair (current_city, remaining_fuel). This state captures all necessary information to determine the number of ways to reach the finish from this point. The transition between states represents moving from one city to another: from state (i, f), we can move to state (j, f - |locations[i] - locations[j]|) for any j != i, provided f >= |locations[i] - locations[j]|. The number of ways for state (i, f) is the sum of ways for all possible next states (j, f - distance). This forms a recurrence relation: dp[i][f] = sum over j != i of dp[j][f - dist(i,j)] if f >= dist(i,j), with base cases dp[finish][any_f] includes 1 (since reaching finish is a valid route, and we can continue). The intuition is that the problem has optimal substructure: the solution for a state depends on solutions of smaller states (with less fuel). This allows using dynamic programming to avoid recomputation. Time complexity to compute all states is O(n² * fuel) as each state (n*fuel) may transition to up to n other states. Space needed is O(n * fuel) to store dp table. Connection to L1: This state-transition model is implemented in the recursive function. Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of fuel consumption. Common mistakes: incorrect state definition (e.g., including path history), missing base cases, or off-by-one errors in fuel indices. Real code pattern: Use 2D array dp[n][fuel+1] initialized to -1 for memoization.

examples.trace: Define dp[i][f] for i=0..2, f=0..4. Base: dp[2][f] = 1 for all f>=0 (finish city). Compute dp[0][4]: From city 0 (loc 1), fuel 4. Transitions: to city 1: dist=|1-3|=2, new fuel=2, add dp[1][2]. to city 2: dist=|1-5|=4, new fuel=0, add dp[2][0]. So dp[0][4] = dp[1][2] + dp[2][0]. Compute dp[1][2]: From city 1 (loc 3), fuel 2. Transitions: to city 0: dist=2, new fuel=0, add dp[0][0]. to city 2: dist=2, new fuel=0, add dp[2][0]. So dp[1][2] = dp[0][0] + dp[2][0]. Compute dp[0][0]: From city 0, fuel 0. Transitions: to city 1: dist=2, invalid; to city 2: dist=4, invalid. So dp[0][0] = 0 (since not finish). dp[2][0] = 1. Thus dp[1][2] = 0 + 1 = 1. dp[0][4] = 1 + 1 = 2.

All locations same, so dist=0 between any cities. dp[1][f] = 1 for all f (finish). Compute dp[0][3]: Transitions: to city 1: dist=0, new fuel=3, add dp[1][3]=1. to city 2: dist=0, new fuel=3, add dp[2][3]. Need dp[2][3]: From city 2, fuel 3. Transitions: to city 0: dist=0, new fuel=3, add dp[0][3] (leads to cycle, handle with memoization). to city 1: dist=0, new fuel=3, add dp[1][3]=1. So dp[2][3] = dp[0][3] + 1. Solve: dp[0][3] = 1 + (dp[0][3] + 1) => dp[0][3] = 1 + dp[0][3] + 1 => 0 = 2, contradiction. Actually, with dist=0, moving between cities doesn't consume fuel, so infinite loops possible. In implementation, we avoid by not moving to same city (next != current), but here from city 0, can move to city 1 and city 2. From city 2, can move to city 0 and city 1. This creates cycles. With memoization, dp[0][3] computed as: dp[0][3] = dp[1][3] + dp[2][3]. dp[1][3]=1. dp[2][3] = dp[0][3] + dp[1][3] = dp[0][3] + 1. So dp[0][3] = 1 + (dp[0][3] + 1) => dp[0][3] = dp[0][3] + 2 => 0=2, invalid. To resolve, in problem, fuel consumption is absolute distance, so if dist=0, moving doesn't use fuel, but we still count routes: from city 0, can go to city 1 directly (1 route), or go to city 2 then to city 1 (1 route), total 2. With DP, we need to handle base: if current==finish, add 1 but also explore. So dp[0][3] = (if 0==finish? no) + sum over next. Let's compute manually: Routes: 0->1, 0->2->1. So dp[0][3]=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Fuel Consumption and Valid Moves
ladders.desc: Calculate fuel needed to move between cities and determine valid moves based on remaining fuel.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves computing the fuel required to travel from one city to another and checking if a move is valid given the remaining fuel. The fuel consumption is defined as the absolute difference between the locations of the two cities: fuel_needed = |locations[i] - locations[j]|. This represents the distance and is always non-negative. A move from city i to city j is valid if: (1) i != j (cannot stay in the same city, as per problem, moving to a different city), and (2) remaining_fuel >= fuel_needed. The intuition is that fuel acts as a resource that depletes with each move, and we must ensure we don't run out of fuel mid-route. This building block is essential for defining state transitions in the DP. The algorithm: given current city index i, remaining fuel f, and list of locations, iterate over all other city indices j (0 to n-1, j != i). For each j, compute dist = abs(locations[i] - locations[j]). If f >= dist, then moving to j is possible, and the new remaining fuel will be f - dist. This step is repeated in the recursive or iterative DP to explore all possibilities. Time complexity for checking moves from one state is O(n) since we check up to n-1 other cities. Space complexity is O(1) for temporary variables. Connection to L2: This is used in the state transitions. Connection to L4: This relies on basic absolute value calculation. Common mistakes: using squared distance instead of absolute value, forgetting to exclude the current city, or incorrect comparison (e.g., f > dist instead of f >= dist). Real code pattern: Loop for j in range(n): if j != i and f >= abs(locations[i]-locations[j]): valid move.

examples.trace: Current city index 0, location 3, fuel 5. Check other cities: City 1: location 7, dist = |3-7| = 4. Since 5 >= 4, move to city 1 is valid, new fuel = 5-4=1. City 2: location 10, dist = |3-10| = 7. Since 5 < 7, move to city 2 is invalid. So only valid move is to city 1.

examples.output: Valid moves: to city 0 and city 1
examples.trace: Current city index 2, location 2, fuel 1. Check other cities: City 0: location 1, dist = |2-1| = 1. Since 1 >= 1, move to city 0 is valid, new fuel = 0. City 1: location 1, dist = |2-1| = 1. Since 1 >= 1, move to city 1 is valid, new fuel = 0. So valid moves to both city 0 and city 1.

testCases.expected: Valid moves: to city 0 and city 1
Valid moves: to city 0 and city 2
Valid moves: to city 1 and city 2
Valid moves: to city 0 and city 2
Valid moves: to city 0, city 1, city 3, city 4

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Absolute Difference Between Locations
ladders.desc: Calculate the absolute difference between two numbers, representing fuel consumption.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation computes the absolute difference between two integers, which in this problem represents the fuel required to travel between two cities. The absolute difference of two numbers a and b is defined as |a - b|, which is a - b if a >= b, or b - a if b > a. It is always non-negative. In programming, this can be done using the abs() function or manual condition checks. The intuition is that fuel consumption is based on distance, which is non-negative and symmetric: moving from city A to city B consumes the same fuel as moving from B to A. This operation is used repeatedly in the algorithm to determine fuel costs for each potential move. The algorithm: given two locations loc_i and loc_j, compute dist = abs(loc_i - loc_j). This dist is then compared to the remaining fuel to check move validity. Time complexity is O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This is used in the fuel consumption calculation. Connection to L5: This builds on basic arithmetic. Common mistakes: using squared difference, forgetting absolute value leading to negative fuel, or integer overflow for large numbers. Real code pattern: dist = abs(locations[i] - locations[j]).

examples.trace: Compute absolute difference: |5 - 9| = | -4 | = 4. Alternatively, since 9 > 5, difference = 9 - 5 = 4.
Compute absolute difference: |7 - 3| = |4| = 4. Alternatively, since 7 > 3, difference = 7 - 3 = 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices, representing cities and their locations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding arrays (or lists) and how to access elements using indices. In this problem, cities are represented by indices from 0 to n-1, where n is the number of cities. Each city has a location stored in an array at the corresponding index. For example, if locations = [2,5,3], then city 0 has location 2, city 1 has location 5, and city 2 has location 3. Accessing an element: use locations[i] to get the location of city i. This is essential for all higher-level operations, such as computing distances between cities. The intuition is that arrays provide a way to map city identifiers (indices) to their properties (locations). The algorithm: declare an array locations of size n; to get location of city i, use locations[i]; to iterate over all cities, use a loop for i in range(n). Time complexity for access is O(1), space O(n) for storing the array. Connection to L4: This provides the inputs for absolute difference calculation. Common mistakes: using 1-based indices incorrectly, accessing out-of-bounds indices, or confusing city index with location value. Real code pattern: locations = [2,5,3]; city_0_loc = locations[0] # returns 2.

examples.trace: Array locations has elements: index 0: 4, index 1: 1, index 2: 7. Accessing locations[1] returns the value at index 1, which is 1.
Array locations: index 0:10, index 1:20, index 2:30. Accessing locations[2] returns 30.
